exemplar.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a sustained focus on the prescribed title and consistently returns to Boxâs aphorism throughout the discussion.
Effectively integrates concrete examples from both mathematics (linear regression, the discriminant) and natural science (food-chain model, ideal-gas law) to support arguments.
Demonstrates awareness of contrasting viewpoints (e.g., theoretical vs practical purposes of a model) and articulates a personal stance in the conclusion.
Makes clear, coherent arguments and links them to areas of knowledge with appropriate illustrations.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive; links to areas of knowledge are not developed beyond surface comparison.
Counter-arguments and alternative viewpoints are asserted rather than critically evaluated or weighed in depth.
Oversimplifications (e.g., âmathematical tools cannot be wrongâ) weaken the coherence of reasoning.
Discussion of implications is limited and does not explore how the studentâs stance affects knowledge production or theory-building.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examinerâs overall mark of 7 lies at the lower boundary of the 7â8 band. The essay demonstrates a clear, sustained focus on the title, coherent argumentation, well-chosen examples across two areas of knowledge, and some evaluation of differing perspectivesâall indicative of strong performance. However, the analysis does not consistently move beyond descriptive comparison into deeper critical evaluation of counterarguments or the implications of the studentâs stance. These limitations prevent a higher band placement. Awarding 7 appropriately reflects performance that exceeds the 5â6 band in focus and coherence but falls short of the fully developed critical depth and exploration of implications characteristic of the 7â8 band.

annotations.comment: Labeling the discriminant as a counterargument to Boxâs claim ignores that its utility is limited to quadratic equations; the student should evaluate its broader implications.
The student clearly identifies the prescribed title and restates it verbatim, demonstrating precise focus on the prompt from the outset.
The attempt to derive a direct contradiction of Boxâs claim is underdeveloped and ends abruptly; it needs a clearer logical progression or illustrative counterexample.
The claim that any mathematical model in use âmust be 'useful'â is asserted without evidence and conflates acceptance with utility; it needs supporting examples or counterevidence.
The final assertion of disagreement lacks reflection on implications for knowledge production; the student could discuss how this stance affects theoryâbuilding.
The introduction restates the question but lacks an explicit thesis or clear stance on the extent of agreement, deferring the personal position until the conclusion.
Definitions of âwrongâ as âinaccurateâ and âusefulâ as predictive are simplistic; there is no critical unpacking of how these terms vary in mathematical versus scientific contexts.
The healthcare example cites failure in COVIDâ19 prediction but does not analyse model assumptions or parameter sensitivity to illustrate why linear regression is inappropriate.
The Atlantic Herring case is specific but lacks analysis of how multiple trophic interactions quantitatively alter model predictions; deeper evaluation of external factors is needed.
The Friedman citation is descriptive and does not analyse how his methodological view directly supports or nuances Boxâs claim, limiting depth of link to the knowledge question.
Conflating the requirement to survive disproof with the model being ârightâ overlooks that many scientific models are provisional; this oversimplification should be critically examined.
Integrating the COVID-19 case study situates the linear modelâs limits within a real scenario; this strengthens the discussion by linking example to the knowledge question.
The assertion that âmathematical tools cannot be wrongâ is an overgeneralisation; it overlooks mathematical modelling limitations and the role of idealisation in proofs.
The discriminant example is well chosen and clearly explained, illustrating how a purely internal variable model achieves theoretical accuracy.
The summary statement is overly general and does not synthesise how the examples from mathematics and natural science converge to address the knowledge question.
The introduction of personal debate in first person is vague; the student should avoid generic placeholders like âmy personal opinionâ and articulate a provisional stance more precisely.
The linear regression example is concrete and appropriate; however, including the regression equation, e.g. $$\,\hat y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$$, would deepen the mathematical analysis.
Stating that âall theories in use today have been provenâ is inaccurate; theories are subject to revision and falsification, especially in natural sciences.
Acknowledging that the food chain model remains useful for teaching by simplifying energy flow is an effective evaluation of its theoretical utility.
The food chain model is a fitting example for natural sciences and its simplification purpose is well articulated in context of theoretical versus realâworld use.
Highlighting that a modelâs usefulness can shift with its intended application (practical vs theoretical) demonstrates awareness of context-dependence.
Emphasising the importance of defining a modelâs purpose is a strong conceptual point that links back to the core of the knowledge question.
The rejection of âall models are wrongâ overlooks models with known theoretical limitations; the student should consider examples of refuted mathematical models.
The critique of âsome models are usefulâ is circular, assuming models in use must be useful; it requires independent evaluation of usefulness criteria.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
